title: The Lost Episodes: Part 1

jokes.text: The host announces Dave Chappelle isn't coming; 'Let's start the show' after a beat of silence
jokes.setup: The show intro music plays, building expectation that Dave Chappelle will appear as normal
jokes.punchline: Brief pause, then matter-of-fact 'I don't think he's coming. Oh. Let's start the show.' — the real-world chaos of Chappelle abandoning the show played as a dry shrug
jokes.explanation: The genius here is that this IS the show's whole premise — Dave really left — and the writers play it with perfect deadpan economy. Three short sentences convey the entire absurdity of the situation. The 'Oh.' beat lands because it performs the audience's own stunned reaction back at them. Scores well on economy and earned quality because it's meta-commentary rooted in real events, not manufactured.

jokes.text: Going once... going twice... Fuck it. Ladies and gentlemen, Donnell Rawlings and Charlie Murphy!
jokes.setup: Standard hype-man intro building to 'Dave Chappelle!' — the audience expects the star
jokes.punchline: Auction-style countdown to nobody, then a resigned 'Fuck it' before pivoting to the replacements
jokes.explanation: The 'going once, going twice' auction framing is a clever structural choice — it literalizes the idea of Dave being sold off or auctioned away, and 'Fuck it' is the perfect resignation beat. Works as meta-commentary and gets the episode off on the right absurdist foot. Slightly docked because the hosts are relatively unknown to the audience so the payoff name-drop has limited punch.

jokes.text: Charlie Murphy: 'Charlie Murphy is not mad at Dave Chappelle. 'Cause if it wasn't for Dave Chappelle, you motherfuckers would still be calling me Eddie Murphy's brother.'
jokes.setup: Charlie opens with magnanimous-sounding defense of Dave — 'Charlie Murphy is not mad' — suggesting nobility
jokes.punchline: The real reason he's not mad is nakedly self-interested: Dave made him famous on his own terms
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's first genuine standout. It only works coming from Charlie Murphy — the self-awareness of being Eddie's lesser-known brother, the honesty about fame being transactional, the implicit shade at Dave all wrapped in two sentences. 'You motherfuckers would still be calling me Eddie Murphy's brother' is the kind of brutally honest self-deprecation that becomes quotable immediately. Character integration is near-perfect: no other human on earth could deliver this line with this specificity. Justifies the 8+ score.

jokes.text: Donnell Rawlings cuts off Charlie mid-speech: 'Nigga, speak for yourself. I need all the camera time I can get, nigga! I'm goin' ass to the glass!'
jokes.setup: Charlie Murphy is giving a relatively composed speech about speaking for both of them
jokes.punchline: Donnell violently rejects the grouping — he's a hungry, lesser-known guy who treats every camera angle as career survival
jokes.explanation: 'Ass to the glass' is a funny, specific, desperate turn of phrase that sells Donnell's hunger perfectly. The joke escalates from interruption to full physical performance (posing for cameras). Works well as character comedy because it reinforces that Donnell has zero chill and everything to prove. Slightly lower than #3 because it's more reactive/complementary.

jokes.text: The sketch setup: Dave on 106 & Park (BET's music video show) downplaying his wealth while a TV in the background announces he just got $55 million.
jokes.setup: Dave tells the 106 & Park host that he 'does all right, nothing to write home about' regarding his cable show income.
jokes.punchline: Simultaneously, the TV behind them announces he received $55 million from Comedy Central. The irony is literalized visually — his humility is exposed as absurd by the TV blaring his actual net worth.
jokes.explanation: Solid structural joke — the simultaneous deflection and exposure works well visually. The timing of the TV announcement undercutting Dave's modest claim is cleanly executed. The real-world resonance (this actually happened roughly) adds depth.

jokes.text: Dave is at a barber telling the barber he 'does all right, nothin' to write home about' on cable — immediately cut away to a news report saying he received $55 million
jokes.setup: Dave humbly downplays his success to the barber — 'it's cable, I do all right'
jokes.punchline: The TV in the barbershop immediately announces '$55 million' — his false modesty explodes in real time
jokes.explanation: Structurally clean — the joke is in the perfect timing of the news report arriving to contradict Dave's lie mid-sentence. The setup earns the punchline. The real-world context (Chappelle actually walking away from $50M) gives it extra resonance. Solid craft.

jokes.text: Barber changes price from $8 to $11,000 the moment Dave is outed as having $55 million
jokes.setup: Barber's chalkboard says $8 for a haircut
jokes.punchline: $11,000 — the moment the money is public, everyone around Dave treats him as an ATM
jokes.explanation: The '$8 to $11,000' jump is funny in its specificity and absurdity — not $100, not $1,000, but $11,000. The 'That's the old price' callback line becomes a running joke for the sketch. Competent sketch construction.

jokes.text: Dave: 'All right. But for $11,000... shave my balls then, nigga. Come on, get to cuttin.'
jokes.setup: Dave reluctantly accepts the extortionate price
jokes.punchline: Instead of refusing, he demands a proportional level of service — a completely absurd escalation
jokes.explanation: Dave's response flips the power dynamic — instead of being victimized by the price hike, he weaponizes it. The specificity of 'shave my balls' is absurd but logically consistent with his character (he's street-smart, not helpless). Gets a strong laugh from its audacity and surprise.

jokes.text: Car mechanic changes $28 price to $873 — same 'that's the old price' excuse
jokes.setup: The barbershop 'that's the old price' joke has established a pattern
jokes.punchline: The mechanic runs the exact same scam, proving it's a universal phenomenon Dave will face everywhere
jokes.explanation: The running gag works because it builds the sketch's thesis (fame = everyone scams you), but it's a straight repeat of the prior joke's logic without adding a new comedic layer. The escalating price jump ($8→$11k vs $28→$873) is actually less extreme, which slightly weakens the escalation.

jokes.text: Dave: 'You think that's the first time I had a gun put in my face? Nigga, that's the sixth time this has happened this week.'
jokes.setup: Dave's got a gun in his face in a tense standoff
jokes.punchline: He's so blasé about it that he's keeping a running count — sixth time this week alone
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'sixth time this week' is what elevates this above a generic 'I'm tough' joke. It implies a whole world of chaos surrounding Dave's wealth that we're only seeing a slice of. The counting makes it absurdist. Works well.

jokes.text: IRS agents reveal themselves; bodyguard turns out to be IRS plant; Dave: 'I pay you $4,000 a week to hold me down, nigga. Hold me down!'
jokes.setup: Dave's trusted bodyguard is present throughout the standoff as protection
jokes.punchline: The bodyguard is revealed to be working for the IRS, and Dave's outrage focuses hilariously on the $4k/week he's been paying a traitor
jokes.explanation: The misdirection works on two levels — the bodyguard twist, and Dave's misplaced priority (fury about the $4k rather than the federal agents). 'Hold me down!' repeated while being literally held down is a nice ironic double meaning. Strong structural joke with good surprise.

jokes.text: Dave fires the bodyguard while IRS agents are still present with guns drawn; 'You know what, dude? You fuckin' fired.'
jokes.setup: Guns drawn, IRS agents surrounding everyone in a tense standoff
jokes.punchline: Dave's priority is HR — he's firing the guy rather than addressing the law enforcement crisis
jokes.explanation: Solid beat — the absurdity of conducting a firing in the middle of a standoff. Slightly undercuts the prior joke's impact rather than building on it, but works as character comedy for Dave's particular brand of oblivious bravado.

jokes.text: Dave threatens the IRS agent: 'When you get out of here, you're in trouble, Jack.' The dying man's response: 'You just gonna let him threaten me like that?'
jokes.setup: Dave is naked and physically pinned by a large man from the IRS
jokes.punchline: Despite being completely powerless, Dave issues a toothless threat; the IRS agent appeals to the bodyguard Dave just fired
jokes.explanation: The impotence of Dave's threat and the IRS agent still caring about it mid-confrontation is funny situational comedy. Medium-tier execution.

jokes.text: IRS agent is shot; dying speech: 'Money... the root of all evil. The IRS pulled the trigger, but your greed did this to me, Dave.'
jokes.setup: A shootout erupts; someone gets hit
jokes.punchline: The dying man's final words are a melodramatic morality speech blaming Dave's greed — completely over-the-top death scene played for dark comedy
jokes.explanation: The dying man launching into a biblical lecture about greed is funny in its commitment and absurd gravity. The sketch earns some dark comedy points here. The 'You didn't have to do two more seasons' follow-up adds a meta layer that sharpens it further (see next joke).

jokes.text: Dying agent: 'You didn't have to do two more seasons no matter how good the show is. They're only gonna say it's not as good as last year was.'
jokes.setup: The dying man is delivering a morality speech about greed and money
jokes.punchline: He pivots to specific show-business advice about sequel diminishing returns — which is also a real meta-commentary on Chappelle's Show S3
jokes.explanation: This earns its 8+ score because it operates on three levels simultaneously: (1) absurdist comedy of a dying man giving career advice, (2) genuine TV industry observation about sequel fatigue, (3) deeply meta commentary — this IS a third season that people would say isn't as good, written into the very episode where it might be said. That layered self-awareness is sophisticated comedy writing. The dying-man delivery makes it land harder than if it were said straight.

jokes.text: Dave: 'You know what the key to keeping the show fresh is?' — dying agent leans in — 'You gotta...' — dies mid-sentence
jokes.setup: Dave desperately asks the dying man for the secret to keeping the show fresh; the agent begins to answer
jokes.punchline: He dies before finishing the sentence — the secret is forever lost
jokes.explanation: Structurally elegant — the withholding of the punchline IS the joke, and it works because the audience genuinely wants the answer too (echoing the real-world mystery of why Chappelle quit). The 'You gotta...' repeated buildup and then death is a perfect comedic construction. The meta dimension (we'll never know Dave's 'secret') makes it resonate beyond the sketch. Economy is near-perfect: the joke is entirely in what is NOT said. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Donnell's 'ka-ching moment': learning Dave went to Africa, he yells 'That mother— I'm broke, bitch!'
jokes.setup: Donnell asks where Dave is; someone says 'Africa. Africa Africa?'
jokes.punchline: Donnell's profane rage reveals his financial dependence on Dave — 'I'm broke, bitch!' is simultaneously his distress call and inadvertent 'Rich bitch' callback
jokes.explanation: 'I'm broke, bitch!' as the inverse of Dave's 'I'm rich, bitch!' catchphrase is a smart piece of writing — it repurposes the show's own iconography to comment on Dave's absence. The 'Africa Africa?' beat of disbelief is also a nice texture. Good character specificity for Donnell.

jokes.text: Donnell reacts to learning Dave went to Africa: 'Africa Africa?' followed by 'That mother—' cut to Donnell screaming 'I'm broke, bitch!'
jokes.setup: Donnell hears Dave left for Africa and is processing this information
jokes.punchline: 'Africa Africa?' as genuine disbelief, then the realization that he personally is now financially ruined
jokes.explanation: 'Africa Africa?' is a genuinely funny doubled question — the distinction between 'Africa' as a metaphor and actual geographical Africa is hilarious in context. The pivot to personal financial devastation is a strong button. Donnell's character consistency throughout the episode pays off here.

jokes.text: Hip-Hop Newsbreak: Anchor Chuck Taylor reads Method Man assault victim's account in straight news format: 'First he put my nuts on the dresser... and then he banged them shits with a spiked bat'
jokes.setup: Donnell sets up 'Hip-Hop Newsbreak' as a parody of news using Wu-Tang lyrics as crime reports. Professional news anchor reads solemnly.
jokes.punchline: The 'victim' recounts the Wu-Tang lyric from Method Man's 'M.E.T.H.O.D. Man' verbatim as if it's a real torture incident, delivered with complete journalistic seriousness
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best sketches. The concept is brilliant: Method Man's famously graphic torture-fantasy lyrics ('put your nuts on the dresser,' 'sewed my asshole shut') read with deadpan news-anchor gravity. The joke works because (a) the lyrics ARE absurdly specific and violent when literalized, (b) the straight-news framing creates maximum comedic contrast, and (c) it's a loving tribute to Wu-Tang that also roasts them. 'This all took place in the slums of Shaolin' listing all Wu-Tang members as witnesses is inspired. The spiked bat 'bow!' sound effect delivered by the victim as testimony is gold. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Victim continues: 'And then he sewed my asshole shut. Kept feeding me and feeding me and feeding me!'
jokes.setup: The previous 'nuts on dresser' revelation has already established the tenor
jokes.punchline: Escalation to the next lyric — the 'feeding' detail is the kicker, the absurd consequence of the previous act
jokes.explanation: Escalation within the bit. The 'feeding' detail is the part that really makes audiences lose it because it reveals the logic of the torture — this is Method Man's genius being appreciated through the comedy lens. The repetition of 'feeding' works well for emphasis.

jokes.text: Chuck Taylor: 'This all took place in the slums of Shaolin. There was the RZA, the GZA, Ol' Dirty Bastard, Raekwon the Chef, U-God, Inspectah Deck, and of course, the Method Man' — listing all Wu-Tang members as witnesses/accomplices
jokes.setup: News report about a Method Man assault is being treated as a genuine crime story
jokes.punchline: The anchor lists all nine Wu-Tang Clan members as present — 'the slums of Shaolin' is Wu-Tang's name for Staten Island, so it's both accurate and insane as a news dateline
jokes.explanation: The roll call of Wu-Tang members as news witnesses is hilarious because it simultaneously sounds like a witness roster in a legitimate news piece and lists them in Wu-Tang album-liner-notes style. 'The slums of Shaolin' as a news dateline is inspired. For Wu-Tang fans, this is wall-to-wall joy.

jokes.text: Chuck Taylor describing Method Man putting a coat hanger on the stove for 40 minutes then 'stuck it in his ass real slow. Like, sss...' — the anchor makes the sizzling sound effect
jokes.setup: Continuing the straight news format for Method Man lyrics
jokes.punchline: The anchor makes the vocal hissing/sizzling sound effect ('sss...') — a newscaster performing sound effects while describing anal torture
jokes.explanation: The commitment to the newscaster voice while producing 'sss...' is peak absurdist comedy. The contrast between journalistic register and content is maximized here. High impact because it's the most visceral/shocking moment of the sketch.

jokes.text: Chuck Taylor: 'At this point, Method Man is still at large, so stay tuned to News Channel 3 for this and other stories. I'm Chuck Taylor.'
jokes.setup: Entire Wu-Tang torture report has been delivered
jokes.punchline: Sign-off is completely professional and by-the-book — 'Method Man is still at large' treated identically to any wanted criminal
jokes.explanation: The formal news sign-off caps the sketch perfectly — maximum deadpan. 'Method Man is still at large' is a funny sentence in any context. The shoe name 'Chuck Taylor' as the anchor is a subtle bonus joke (Chuck Taylor = Converse sneakers) that rewards attentive viewers.

jokes.text: Donnell: '$50 million out of nowhere — would you use it to get revenge on somebody? Dave Chappelle felt the same way.' Premise setup for revenge sketch
jokes.setup: Donnell asks the audience a rhetorical question about revenge fantasies
jokes.punchline: The reveal that this is specifically Dave's psychology — a $50M man who immediately uses it for petty revenge — characterizes him perfectly
jokes.explanation: Good framing for the revenge sketch — 'petty revenge' as the first use of $50M is a funny character beat for Dave. The intro does its job efficiently.

jokes.text: Dave shows up at ex-girlfriend's house with bodyguards, looking extremely wealthy; her son answers the door, Dave asks where his father works: 'He works at the doughnut shop on Roscoe Boulevard.' Dave: 'Sounds lucrative.'
jokes.setup: Dave has shown up to impress/devastate his ex; he's learning intel from her young son
jokes.punchline: 'Sounds lucrative' — dripping with contempt for the doughnut shop while keeping a friendly tone in front of the child
jokes.explanation: Two-word putdown with perfect comic economy. Dave maintains surface politeness to a child while the subtext is pure contempt — the word 'lucrative' doing enormous satirical work. Strong quotability.

jokes.text: Dave reveals ex cheated on him 'with Jamal, of all people' on the night he asked her to marry him — and this gave him 'trust issues I struggle with to this very day'
jokes.setup: Ex-girlfriend apologizes, says 'we were all just kids'
jokes.punchline: Dave delivers an unnervingly specific and emotionally detailed accounting of the betrayal — 'Jamal, of all people' — the 'of all people' implying Jamal is particularly humiliating as the choice
jokes.explanation: 'Jamal, of all people' is a perfectly deployed specificity. The 'of all people' does character work without over-explaining — we intuit that Jamal is either a friend, inferior option, or both. The trust issues admission makes Dave simultaneously petty and sympathetic, which is good character writing.

jokes.text: Ex: 'The good news is, Jamal and I are still together, and he's legit now.'
jokes.setup: Ex seems to be trying to relate to Dave, offering an update
jokes.punchline: 'He's legit now' — the 'now' implying he was previously not legit, which is both specific shade and character revelation about her taste in men
jokes.explanation: 'He's legit now' is quietly funny — it implies a whole criminal history delivered as a side note while trying to present good news. Economy works well; character-specific for Shonda as someone with questionable judgment.

jokes.text: Dave proposes to ex — 'I want you to marry me' — then when she hesitates about his wife, he dismisses his wife with 'Her? It's over between us.' and her husband with 'What about him? Leave him. You don't need his doughnut shop money, I got you.'
jokes.setup: Dave makes a passionate, sincere-seeming marriage proposal
jokes.punchline: The rapid-fire dismissal of both spouses — 'Her?' about his own wife, then 'doughnut shop money' as the ultimate economic insult — exposes the whole thing as an elaborate financial flex
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the doughnut shop established earlier. 'Her?' as a two-letter dismissal of his wife is funny in its contemptuous brevity. The marriage proposal as economic humiliation device is a solid character beat for Dave.

jokes.text: Dave reveals the engagement was a setup: 'This is my wife and my son! Girl, I didn't get no divorce — we're happy as hell!'
jokes.setup: Dave appeared to be genuinely pursuing the ex and promising divorce
jokes.punchline: The whole proposal was an elaborate trap — wife and son were waiting to witness Shonda's humiliation
jokes.explanation: Strong misdirection payoff — the setup invested real emotional time in the proposal so the reveal lands with genuine surprise. 'We're happy as hell!' while his family stands there grinning is a great visual. The willingness of his family to participate in the revenge is its own funny detail (becomes explicit in the next joke). High impact because it recontextualizes everything before it.

jokes.text: Dave reveals the setup: 'This my wife and my son! Girl, I didn't get no divorce — we're happy as hell!'
jokes.setup: Extended romantic reconciliation scene — the ex believes Dave genuinely wants her back
jokes.punchline: The entire courtship was a revenge prank — Dave's real wife walks out to help humiliate the ex
jokes.explanation: The reveal is well-earned — the long slow-burn courtship makes the punchline land harder. 'We're happy as hell' is a great line because it doesn't just deny the divorce, it asserts genuine happiness as the final twist of the knife. Clean misdirection.

jokes.text: Dave: 'Why did you do this to me?' — 'Cause I'm petty and lucky for me, my family's petty too.'
jokes.setup: Shonda asks why Dave would do this elaborate revenge scheme
jokes.punchline: Dave gives a completely honest, self-aware answer: 'I'm petty' — no justification, no higher moral ground, just proud pettiness
jokes.explanation: This earns its 8+ score. 'I'm petty and lucky for me, my family's petty too' is a quotable, character-perfect line that works on multiple levels: (1) it's a funny reversal of 'we're petty but at least we have each other' family values, (2) it's a perfect character statement — Dave's superpower is boundless self-awareness, (3) the 'lucky for me' framing is a fresh angle on pettiness-as-virtue. The economy is near-perfect. This line could become a cultural reference. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Dave's wife and son mock Shonda's squirrel skin coat: 'Nice shoes.' / 'Look at that squirrel skin coat. Get that coat some peanuts.'
jokes.setup: Dave just revealed the revenge — now his family piles on
jokes.punchline: The son's 'Get that coat some peanuts' is a specific, inventive squirrel-coat insult that shows the whole family has the same comedic DNA
jokes.explanation: 'Get that coat some peanuts' is a genuinely creative insult — funny because it's childlike in logic but devastating in effect. The family's synchronized pettiness escalates the scene nicely. The coat insult works better than a generic 'your clothes are ugly' line because it commits to the specific squirrel detail.

jokes.text: Dave: 'Let's go to the amusement park. I don't know about you, but I want some doughnuts.'
jokes.setup: The revenge is complete; they're leaving the ex humiliated
jokes.punchline: Doughnut reference as a final twist of the knife — referencing the husband's doughnut shop as Dave exits triumphantly
jokes.explanation: Good callback execution — the doughnut reference lands because it's been built through two prior mentions. It's the joke version of pouring salt in the wound. The casual, cheerful tone while referencing the husband's inferior livelihood is the right register.

jokes.text: The family leaves to the amusement park, specifically calling out: 'I don't know about you, but I want some doughnuts.'
jokes.setup: Dave's family is heading out and Dave suggests activities.
jokes.punchline: Suggesting doughnuts as their celebratory treat is a direct callback to the husband's doughnut shop — they're going to eat the competition's product in the wake of their victory. The choice of doughnuts specifically to twist the knife.
jokes.explanation: The doughnut callback as the exit line is a well-constructed button. The choice is specific enough that it clearly isn't accidental — it's one final twist. Good structural awareness.

jokes.text: Dave's son: 'How many squirrels must die for you to look fly?'
jokes.setup: The family has been mocking Shonda's coat on the way out
jokes.punchline: The son delivers a rhyming, PETA-style condemnation of Shonda's fashion — 'look fly' rhyming with 'must die' in a protest-slogan cadence from a child
jokes.explanation: This earns its 8+ score. 'How many squirrels must die for you to look fly?' has near-perfect economy — ten words that simultaneously work as rhyming wordplay, fashion critique, absurdist PETA parody, and character comedy for a child who has fully absorbed his father's brand of petty wit. The rhyme scheme makes it quotable and the PETA-slogan cadence gives it an extra layer of absurdist comedy. Coming from a child makes it funnier still. One of the episode's sharpest single lines.

jokes.text: Dave drives away, rolls window down: 'Hey Shonda — I'm rich, bitch!'
jokes.setup: Entire revenge sketch has been building; we've heard 'I'm broke, bitch!' as the inverse earlier
jokes.punchline: Dave's signature catchphrase deployed as the revenge's final punctuation
jokes.explanation: The catchphrase as a sketch ending is well-earned here — unlike many uses of a catchphrase as lazy punctuation, this one has been built toward through the entire sketch. The character address ('Hey Shonda') makes it specific rather than generic. Donnell's earlier 'I'm broke, bitch!' inversion makes this callback resonate on two levels. Maximum economy (three words), maximum character integration. High impact in a live setting.

jokes.text: Dave arrives at Steve's club for revenge; describes having gasoline with him to burn it down, then apologizes: 'You don't smell that gas?'
jokes.setup: Dave has arrived appearing friendly; Steve gives a heartfelt apology for banning him from the club years ago
jokes.punchline: Dave admits he came to commit arson and apparently brought the gas already — the apology has temporarily disrupted his plan
jokes.explanation: 'You don't smell that gas?' is a great line because it reveals that Dave is completely sincere — he genuinely brought the gas and is now genuinely conflicted by Steve's apology. The directness of the confession is funnier than if it were more devious. Good dark comedy.

jokes.text: The Super Bowl ad: 'Hey, I'm Dave Chappelle, and I'm here to say a few words about America's premiere casting agent. Of course I'm talking about Jerry Jacobs. She sucks ass!'
jokes.setup: Infomercial/testimonial format with Dave as earnest spokesperson
jokes.punchline: The 'premiere casting agent' build is immediately torched by 'she sucks ass'
jokes.explanation: The fake-testimonial structure is used brilliantly here — every positive framing device (spokesperson format, 'of course I'm talking about') makes the punchline more devastating. The formality of the Super Bowl ad format heightens the absurdity of using it for a personal vendetta. Strong structural craft.

jokes.text: Ad claims Jerry called Dave 'a nigga' on the set of Jungle Fever. Jerry: 'I never said that.' Ad: 'Kiss my black ass, Jerry. Two times!' followed by another person giving Dave dap.
jokes.setup: Ad making allegations against Jerry; she's watching and can directly contradict it
jokes.punchline: Her denial is immediately steamrolled by 'Kiss my black ass, Jerry. Two times!' — plus the 'Give me some' dap from a third party as social confirmation
jokes.explanation: 'Two times' after 'kiss my black ass' is a great escalation within the beat. The dap-giver as silent endorser of the racism allegation is a funny visual element. The Jungle Fever reference is specific and period-appropriate.

jokes.text: Ad outro: 'You can see more of Jerry in this month's Cameltoe Magazine.' Jerry: 'Oh, my God, Jerry.' Dave: 'Got some bad news for you. You're fuckin' done, son! It's over.' ¶ A moment like this ¶
jokes.setup: The ad has already been devastating on professional and racial grounds
jokes.punchline: Final degradation: Cameltoe Magazine — pure gratuitous humiliation, followed by Kelly Clarkson playing over Dave's gloating
jokes.explanation: Cameltoe Magazine is a cheap joke but 'You're fuckin' done, son' with triumphant Kelly Clarkson music is a genuinely funny comedic punctuation. The music choice is inspired — the sincerity of 'A Moment Like This' contrasted with Dave's petty victory is perfect.

jokes.text: Steve the club owner reveals he's in a wheelchair from a bad car accident 'not long after you left here.' Then: 'Jay Rizzo — you remember him? He's in a wheelchair too.'
jokes.setup: Dave comes to burn down the club of the man who banned him
jokes.punchline: Steve is already in a wheelchair — karma has arrived before Dave could; then Jay Rizzo is also in a wheelchair — the universe is on a roll
jokes.explanation: The escalation of wheelchair victims is darkly funny. Two is funnier than one. The casual 'he's in a wheelchair too, man' implies a whole community of the universe's punished wrongdoers.

jokes.text: Steve gives heartfelt apology; Dave gets emotional, says the apology lifts weight off his shoulders — then reveals: 'Thank you. Thank you for making this that much sweeter, you son of a bitch.'
jokes.setup: Dave appears to have been moved by Steve's genuine apology and may abandon revenge
jokes.punchline: The sincerity of the apology hasn't defused Dave — it's made the revenge BETTER by adding emotional stakes. 'Thank you' pivots from gratitude to predator satisfaction
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best moments. The structure is excellent — Dave's 'thank you' runs through several repetitions that sound like genuine emotional release, then the 'you son of a bitch' pivot recontextualizes every prior 'thank you' as sinister. The idea that sincere apology makes revenge sweeter (not weaker) is a genuinely funny and psychologically true comedic insight. 'That much sweeter' is doing a lot of work — it implies Dave's revenge was planned, calculated, and the apology is the cherry on top. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Dave pushes Steve (in a wheelchair) down the stairs; Steve: 'Hey, hey! What the fuck, man? Not the stairs, man!'
jokes.setup: Dave has just delivered the 'that much sweeter' line; Steve is in a wheelchair at the top of a staircase
jokes.punchline: Dave pushes Steve down the stairs — the revenge was apparently wheelchair-shoving — and Steve's 'Not the stairs, man!' implies this is specifically worse than other options
jokes.explanation: The wheelchair-down-stairs gag is the most viscerally shocking comedy moment of the episode. It gets a big shocked laugh on impact. Slightly docked on 'earned vs. cheap' because pushing a disabled person down stairs is a fairly blunt instrument — the comedy is more from audacity than craft. 'Not the stairs, man!' from Steve lands well because it acknowledges other revenge options might have been acceptable, which is absurd. High impact, moderate craft.

jokes.text: Dave looks down at Steve (who just tumbled down the stairs) and says: 'Oh, I love it when a plan comes together.'
jokes.setup: Steve has just been thrown down a flight of stairs
jokes.punchline: Dave's A-Team quote delivered with complete satisfaction — treating wheelchair-rolling as a smooth, sophisticated plan
jokes.explanation: The A-Team reference adds a pop-culture layer, but it's a fairly well-worn phrase. The comedy is more in the context (stairs/wheelchair) than the quote's craft. Functional capper to the scene.

jokes.text: After intense revenge sketch, Dave plays with a baby backstage; baby is cute; Dave: 'Oh! That little motherfucker, bye!'
jokes.setup: Tonal whiplash from the dark revenge sketch — Dave is now cooing over a baby
jokes.punchline: 'That little motherfucker, bye!' — Dave's term of endearment for the baby is entirely profane, which is funny because it's genuine affection expressed in Dave's natural register
jokes.explanation: 'That little motherfucker, bye!' is a perfectly Dave Chappelle phrase — the profanity as term of endearment is character-perfect. Also serves as tonal relief after the dark revenge sequences. The audience laughs at the contrast between the word and the sentiment.

jokes.text: Donnell setup for Tupac sketch: 'You ever been in the club and a new Tupac record comes out? It's — he's living!'
jokes.setup: Donnell describes the audience reaction to posthumous Tupac releases at clubs
jokes.punchline: 'He's living!' — the collective delusion of Tupac fans convinced posthumous releases prove he's alive, played as crowd psychology observation
jokes.explanation: Good observational premise that earns the Tupac sketch. The Elvis comparison ('If Elvis is alive, Tupac is alive!') and the specific detail of 'Tupac on 42nd Street selling Biggie t-shirts two for $10' are funny image details. The t-shirt detail is particularly good — Tupac selling merch for his rival is the perfect absurd scenario.

jokes.text: Donnell: 'I saw Tupac on 42nd Street selling Biggie t-shirts two for $10, man.'
jokes.setup: The premise that Tupac is alive and living in hiding
jokes.punchline: He's specifically selling BIGGIE t-shirts — his murdered rival — which suggests either profound irony, financial desperation, or both
jokes.explanation: This is an excellent single-line joke. The specificity of 'selling Biggie t-shirts two for $10' is genuinely inventive — it subverts in two directions simultaneously: the absurdity of Tupac being alive AND the irony of him selling rival merch. 'Two for $10' is the perfect price point — cheap enough to be sad, specific enough to be real. This joke is quotable and original. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Donnell: 'Let's just say his lyrics were a little bit before their time.' Sets up the fake Tupac track.
jokes.setup: Reference to Tupac's posthumous recording catalog being voluminous
jokes.punchline: The polite understatement 'a little bit before their time' is setting up the reveal that the lyrics reference things that happened after Tupac's death
jokes.explanation: Clean transitional setup. The understatement works because we know what's coming — it sets up the sketch beautifully.

jokes.text: Tupac's 'new' song casually drops 'from Skytel to BlackBerry pagers' and 'Run up in your spot like C.J. from San Andreas' — modern references in a song claimed to be from 1994
jokes.setup: The song is being presented as a genuine unreleased Tupac song 'from 1994'
jokes.punchline: References to BlackBerry pagers (2003-era tech) and Grand Theft Auto: San Andreas (2004 video game) make the lie hilariously transparent
jokes.explanation: The anachronistic references are the central mechanism of the bit and they're well-chosen — BlackBerry and GTA:SA are specific enough to be funny and current enough to be obviously fake. The song's meta-self-awareness ('I wrote this song a long time ago') is itself part of the joke. Good concept execution.

jokes.text: Tupac song: '...better choke him 'cause he's a snitch / I'm talking about George W. Smith / from City Council / he ran in '93 out in Oakland / you probably didn't hear about him'
jokes.setup: The song appears to be dissing President George W. Bush ('Half the people voted for George W.')— a topical political reference
jokes.punchline: Rapid pivot: 'I'm talking about George W. Smith from City Council' — a nobody in Oakland from 1993, deliberately undercutting the political reading with mundane specificity
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's cleverest joke. The misdirection is expert-level: the audience is primed to hear a Tupac diss of President Bush (a politically satisfying read), then the rug pull reveals an obscure Oakland city council candidate from 1993 — which (a) reinforces the '1994' lie while simultaneously destroying it, (b) subverts political comedy expectations, (c) 'you probably didn't hear about him' is perfectly self-aware, and (d) it still functions as commentary on the Tupac-alive mythology because posthumous rap songs ARE often misread as topical. The level of misdirection sophistication here is unusually high for the episode. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Tupac mid-song: '¶ how am I doing this? ¶'
jokes.setup: The song has been maintaining the premise of being a 1994 recording
jokes.punchline: Tupac, mid-verse, breaks character to wonder aloud how he's rapping about future events from the past — the supernatural impossibility of the premise acknowledged by the ghost himself
jokes.explanation: Four words, maximum impact. 'How am I doing this?' delivered in the voice of a dead man rapping about the future is a spectacular meta-comedic moment. It works as (1) genuine absurdist self-questioning, (2) a winking acknowledgment of the sketch's premise, (3) a funny disruption of rap's usual bravado register. Perfect economy — this is what 10/10 economy looks like. The brevity makes it land harder than any explanation could. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Tupac raps about a specific girl in the club whose miniskirt doesn't match her shirt, and there's 'a pudding stain on the back — what the fuck is that? It might be doo-doo'
jokes.setup: Tupac's song has been addressing large historical/political themes
jokes.punchline: He pivots to forensic observation of a woman's outfit in the specific club he's playing in — 'pudding stain... might be doo-doo' — hyperlocal commentary from beyond the grave
jokes.explanation: The pivot from global historical references to a fashion critique of someone standing ten feet away is a brilliant comedic scale shift. 'Pudding stain... might be doo-doo' is delightfully specific and juvenile coming from a supposedly prophetic dead rapper. The uncertainty ('might be') makes it funnier. The audience in the actual club being called out is a great meta-comedy moment.

jokes.text: Tupac directly addresses someone at a table: 'You in the back, you ain't shit / you bought a gin and tonic but you didn't even tip / and if you hit this table one more time the record might skip'
jokes.setup: The Tupac song is supposedly a prophetic 1994 recording
jokes.punchline: The ghost of Tupac directly addresses a specific cheapskate audience member who won't stop bumping the DJ table — real-time grievances from beyond the grave about table etiquette
jokes.explanation: The escalation of specificity reaches peak absurdity here — Tupac calling out one person's failure to tip while simultaneously warning about the DJ setup is surreal genius. The 'record might skip' line is self-aware about the song being a recording while commenting on the physical turntable. 'You ain't shit' as a lyric about bad tipping behavior from a legendary rapper is funny on every level. The specificity (gin and tonic, no tip, table-hitting) makes it feel improvised and live, adding to the effect. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Fake Tupac: '¶ Dave Chappelle / that ain't your wife / damn, man, go back to your kids / go home ¶'
jokes.setup: Tupac's song has been addressing crowd members directly
jokes.punchline: Tupac now addresses Dave Chappelle specifically — calling out that whoever Dave is with at the club isn't his wife and telling him to go home to his kids
jokes.explanation: Tupac using his posthumous track to give Dave Chappelle personal life advice is a perfect meta escalation. The specificity ('that ain't your wife') implies real knowledge about Dave's life, and 'go back to your kids' as Tupac's parting wisdom to the show's creator is both funny and strangely touching. 'Damn, man, go home' as Tupac-as-life-coach is a great character beat.

jokes.text: Tupac: '¶ that ain't your wife / damn, man, go back to your kids ¶' — addressing Dave Chappelle specifically by name in the song
jokes.setup: Tupac has been making hyperlocal observations about the club
jokes.punchline: He calls out Dave Chappelle by name in the song, implying Dave is at the club with someone who isn't his wife — a meta-commentary reaching into the real world
jokes.explanation: Tupac calling out Dave Chappelle by name in his own show is a fun meta-layer. 'Damn, man, go back to your kids' as moral advice from a dead rapper to the show's host is playfully subversive. The wife setup in the revenge sketch earlier gives this slight extra resonance (we know Dave's wife and family).

jokes.text: Tupac: '¶ way before beanie sigel had to do a bid / way before Dave Chappelle had two kids / don't give him no hoochie ¶'
jokes.setup: The song's recurring 'I wrote this a long time ago' refrain keeps adding anachronisms
jokes.punchline: Dave Chappelle's children referenced as timeline evidence — his personal life as a data point proving the song is fake
jokes.explanation: Continuing the meta-Dave thread — 'Dave Chappelle had two kids' as a timestamp works as an extension of the running anachronism joke. 'Don't give him no hoochie' as protective advice from Tupac is funny given the wife/cheating themes of the earlier sketch.

jokes.text: Tupac ends song: '¶ Tupac is raw / I wrote this rhyme in 1994 / I'm not alive ¶'
jokes.setup: The entire conceit has been maintaining ambiguity about whether Tupac is alive
jokes.punchline: 'I'm not alive' — Tupac explicitly, matter-of-factly confirms his own death mid-rhyme, which is both a deflation of the 'Tupac is alive' conspiracy and a surreal statement from the character
jokes.explanation: Three words that cap the entire sketch perfectly. 'I'm not alive' is funny in its directness — the conspiracy theory punctured by the man himself. The rhyme scheme (raw/1994/not alive) makes it feel like a proper lyrical ending while delivering an absurdist bomb. Perfect economy. The matter-of-factness of the statement ('I'm not alive') after the entire sketch has been playing with the conceit is earned and satisfying. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: After the fake Tupac song ends, Donnell says 'Tupac, rest in peace.' Beat. Tupac: '¶ okay, I will ¶'
jokes.setup: Respectful RIP sign-off that would normally end the bit
jokes.punchline: Tupac responds — from whatever plane of existence he's on — with cheerful compliance, confirming he's been present and listening the whole time
jokes.explanation: The best joke in the episode and one of the best closers in Chappelle's Show sketch history. The structure is impeccable: 'Tupac, rest in peace' is the perfect straight-line because it's a genuine ritual sign-off that seems to END the joke. The post-credits-style '¶ okay, I will ¶' from Tupac demolishes the fourth wall completely — he's been alive, listening, and cheerfully compliant with the instruction to die. The economy (three words) makes it land like a perfect punch. It simultaneously confirms and parodies the conspiracy theory. This is what a 9 looks like.

jokes.text: Donnell: 'Tupac, rest in peace.' — Song responds: '¶ okay, I will ¶'
jokes.setup: Donnell gives the formal R.I.P. signoff
jokes.punchline: 'Okay, I will' — the dead man's casual, cooperative acknowledgment of his own death as though it's a pleasant suggestion
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best joke. 'Okay, I will' as a response to 'Rest in peace' is a masterpiece of comedic economy and timing. It works as: (1) literal acceptance of the R.I.P. instruction — cheerful compliance with death, (2) perfect comic capper after the 'I'm not alive' admission that precedes it, (3) subversive commentary on the R.I.P. ritual — we say it reflexively but here the recipient responds, (4) pure surreal comedy — the dead giving consent to their rest. The surprise of the song responding where silence was expected maximizes impact. Ten syllables, maximum comedy. This is what a 9+ joke looks like in this episode. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Outtake/blooper: Charlie Murphy attempting to film the Method Man newscast victim testimony, cracking up before finishing 'method man put a hanger on the stove.'
jokes.setup: The newscast bit has already aired; now we see the outtake of it being filmed
jokes.punchline: Charlie Murphy cannot maintain his composure reading the Wu-Tang torture verse, cracking up before 'left it for like 40 minutes'
jokes.explanation: Outtakes are charming but structurally the weakest type of comedy — you're laughing at the person laughing at their own joke rather than the joke itself. Still, Charlie Murphy's genuine corpsing at the material confirms the bit's quality. Rewatch bonus because seeing what breaks a veteran like Charlie Murphy is interesting.

tmdb_overview: Dave looks to enact vengeance on everyone who has done him wrong in the past after signing a sweet new deal. Meanwhile, people suddenly charge him a lot more money for even the most trivial things that used to cost very little.
